elements.sent-2411-001: Quadruped robots with manipulators hold promise for extending the workspace and enabling robust locomotion, but existing results do not investigate such a capability.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper proposes \acronym{ with three components to address these issues: (1) adaptation of learned low-level controller for VR-enabled whole-body teleoperation and traversability; (2) \acronym{-Skill --- a library of generalizable visuomotor skills acquired via imitation learning or heuristics and (3) \acronym{-Planner --- an interface of learned skills that allow LLM planners to coordinate skills for long-horizon tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: We demonstrate the importance of high-quality training data by achieving higher grasping success rate over existing RL baselines using only tens of demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Besides extensive quantitative evaluation, we qualitatively demonstrate practical robot applications, such as cleaning up trash in university hallways or outdoor terrains, operating articulated objects, and rearranging items on a bookshelf.
elements.sent-2411-005: Practical robot mobile manipulation requires generalizable skills and long-horizon task execution.
elements.sent-2411-006: Consider a scenario where a mobile robot is deployed out-of-box at a family house.
elements.sent-2411-007: The robot is tasked with daily chores including collecting the trash around the house and grabbing something for human.
elements.sent-2411-008: To accomplish these tasks, the robot needs skills that generalize to unseen objects and a planner capable of compositing skills over a long horizon.
elements.sent-2411-009: Existing methods~\cite{liu2024-vbc,liu2024-okrobot,qiu2024-geff,zhi2024-comerobot,fu2024mobilealoha,gu2024-conceptgraphs, xiong2024adaptive have approached mobile manipulation from two primary directions.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Modular methods~\cite{liu2024-okrobot, qiu2024-geff, zhi2024-comerobot aim at designing decoupled perception-planning modules.}
elements.sent-2411-011: With advances in large-scale vision models~\cite{kirillov2023segment, radford2021-CLIP, liu2023-groundingdino , recent modular methods~\cite{qiu2024-geff,liu2024-okrobot exhibit strong generalizability in perception to an open set of language-specified objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, their planning modules~\cite{chang2023goat,liu2024-okrobot,qiu2024-geff, gu2024-conceptgraphs often rely on heuristic-based motion planning, limiting tasks to mostly simple pick-and-place.}
elements.sent-2411-013: End-to-end approaches~\cite{liu2024-vbc,fu2024mobilealoha,zhao2023aloha,ha2024-umionlegs,chi2024-umi-gripper,chi2023-diffusionpolicy , on the other hand, use learned policies to enable robot with complex actions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: They, however, often hold a strong assumption of the small training-testing distribution gap ({\it e.g., sim2real~\cite{liu2024-vbc or intra-class variation~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha ) and thus do not show strong generalizability.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition, policies learned via imitation learning are prone to compounding error~\cite{zhao2023aloha,iyer2024-openteach over long-horizon execution.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, these learned skills should be designed to be as atomic as possible for both generalizability and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper investigates in-the-wild mobile manipulation that addresses these issues for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, in-the-wild manipulation requires the robot to have skills that (1) generalize across texture, lighting, and diverse environments; (2) are capable of long-horizon execution; and (3) perform complex manipulation beyond pick-and-place.
elements.sent-2411-020: For generalizability, \acronym{ enables language-conditioned imitation learning (\acronym{ -Skill).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Building upon ACT~\cite{zhao2023aloha, fu2024mobilealoha , \acronym{ -Skill improves generalizability via pre-trained CLIP and composable skills.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Instead of simply using CLIP features~\cite{chi2024-umi-gripper,ha2024-umionlegs , we apply a reparameterization trick~\cite{zhou2022-MASKCLIP to CLIP to compute probability maps given object text query as an auxiliary input.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We then use VR teleoperation~\cite{cheng2024-opentv, ding2024-bunnyvisionpro to collect human demonstrations to acquire complex skills such as non-prehensile manipulation.}
elements.sent-2411-024: We adapt a learned low-level controller~\cite{liu2024-vbc for VR-based whole-body teleoperation, which significantly increases the robot workspace and reduces the demonstration cost by 26.9\% compared to the decoupled strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, based on a library of acquired generalizable and atomic skills, \acronym{ provides a language interface (\acronym{ -Planner) that allows interfacing with LLMs to composite skills for long-horizon execution.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: A generic framework with techniques that allow generalizable language-condition imitation learning (\acronym{-Skill ) with interfacing to the LLM planner (\acronym{-Planner ).}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Demonstrations of in-the-wild mobile manipulation tasks with full-stack and systematic deployment of the proposed framework.
elements.sent-2411-029: Comprehensive evaluation and ablation for the proposed technique, which paves the way for future study.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.96\textwidth]{imgs/overview_icra_final.pdf} \caption{\textbf{Overview of \acronym{} models and robot setups.} (a) \acronym{} takes a frozen CLIP model to encode task-specific texts and visual observations; (b) Our robot platform is a Unitree B1 quadruped combined with a Unitree Z1 arm and a 3D-printed gripper, with two RGBD cameras and one lidar mounted on. } \label{fig:clipact_wholebody_overview}
elements.sent-2411-030: Mobile manipulation has gained increasing attention for its vision of enabling robots to perform diverse practical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: In terms of hardware configurations, wheeled robots have made substantial strides~\cite{wu2023tidybot,xiong2024adaptive,sun2022fully,ahn2022can,lew2023robotic,liu2024-okrobot,zhi2024-comerobot for its reliable base movement~\cite{hellorobot-website , while recently, legged robots have also gained more interest for its robust locomotion~\cite{cheng2024-extremeparkour,yang2023-legged-prior and the extended workspace via whole-body arm-base coordination~\cite{liu2024-vbc,ha2024-umionlegs,portela2024-compliant,fu2023deep .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Categorized by methodology, existing methods can be divided into modular methods and end-to-end methods.
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent modular approaches~\cite{liu2024-okrobot,zhi2024-comerobot,qiu2024-geff,zhang2023gamma,yokoyama2023asc,arcari2023bayesian,zhang2024-adaptigraph,ji2024graspsplats,liu2024-dynamem design decoupled perception-planning strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In particular, perception~\cite{liu2024-okrobot,qiu2024-geff,zhi2024-comerobot are often done by applications of vision foundation models~\cite{gu2021open,kirillov2023segment,radford2021-CLIP,liu2023-groundingdino ; whereas grasping are done by off-the-shelf pose prediction models ({\it e.g., GraspNet~\cite{fang2020-graspnet ) and IK solver~\cite{rosmoveit .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Despite strong perception designs, modular methods are mostly limited to simple pick-and-place tasks.
elements.sent-2411-036: On the other hand, end-to-end approaches use Reinforcement Learning (RL)~\cite{gu2023multiskill,xia2021relmogen,xiong2024adaptive,liu2024-vbc,pan2024-roboduet or Imitation Learning (IL)~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,shafiullah2023bringing,ha2024-umionlegs to enable complex tasks beyond pick-and-place such as articulated manipulation~\cite{xiong2024adaptive,bharadhwaj2024-track2act and non-prehensile manipulation~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,ha2024-umionlegs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, these work often fall short when training-testing distributions mismatch.
elements.sent-2411-038: Most closely related to our work, \citet{yokoyama2023asc proposed to use sim2real RL for in-the-wild mobile manipulation.}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, they do not investigate manipulation tasks other than simple pick-and-place.
elements.sent-2411-040: For robots to assist with real-world tasks such as cleaning up home, they need to be capable of dealing with long-horizon mobile manipulation, where independent skills are planned and triggered to complete given goals.
elements.sent-2411-041: Existing methods rely on sampling-based planning~\cite{siddharth2014-interfacedplanning,garrett2020pddlstream , RL~\cite{yokoyama2023asc,yenamandra2023-homerobot,liang2024-skilldiffuser,gu2023multiskill , and Large Language Models (LLMs)~\cite{rana2023-sayplan,song2023-llm-planner,huang2022-zeroshot-planner,gu2024-conceptgraphs to coordinate skill primitives for long-horizon task execution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Recent work~\cite{rana2023-sayplan,song2023-llm-planner,huang2022-zeroshot-planner,gu2024-conceptgraphs have found that LLM-based methods, especially Large-Mutlimodal Models (LMMs)~\cite{cheng2024spatialrgpt,openai2023gpt4 , are promising to serve as effective planners for embodied agents, where the research efforts are centered around hierarchical search~\cite{rana2023-sayplan and re-planning~\cite{zhi2024-comerobot .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Instead of studying the planning capability, we investigate the potential of interfacing LMMs with skills acquired via imitation learning for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-044: Imitation learning has demonstrated promising results through learning from real-world expert demonstrations~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,wong2022error,shafiullah2023bringing,chi2023-diffusionpolicy,ha2024-umionlegs,cheng2024-opentv,yang2024-ace,ding2024-bunnyvisionpro .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Investigated for decades since the 80s~\cite{pomerleau1988alvinn , behavior cloning~\cite{pomerleau1988alvinn,bojarski2016end is one of the most commonly used imitation learning approach that learns an end-to-end mapping from observations to actions.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Recently, researchers have shown that this classic approach not only allows complex manipulations~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,cheng2024-opentv,ding2024-bunnyvisionpro,zhao2024-alohaunleashed , but also holds the potential that scaling up training data with low-cost hardware~\cite{chi2024-umi-gripper,ha2024-umionlegs,yang2024-ace,zhao2023aloha,wu2023-gello,shafiullah2023bringing,fu2024mobilealoha will lead to generalizable policies.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Similar to existing work~\cite{ding2024-bunnyvisionpro,cheng2024-opentv,shen2023-f3rm,qin2023-anyteleop,he2024omnih2o , we also use VR devices to collect expert demonstrations that minimize the expert-agent observation gap~\cite{zhao2023aloha .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: To reduce the cross-embodiment gap between the human operator and the quadruped robot, we combine the VR demonstration with learned whole-body controller.
elements.sent-2411-049: In addition, we also improve the vanilla ACT model~\cite{zhao2023aloha,fu2024mobilealoha to support language-conditioned imitation learning that is more generalizable with autonomous termination.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Quadruped Whole-Body Control (WBC) draws inspiration from the natural motions of animals to extend the robot workspace via arm-base coordination.
elements.sent-2411-051: The WBC capability is usually achieved via model-based hierarchical trajectory optimization~\cite{bellicoso2019alma, ma2022combining, sleiman2023versatile, zimmermann2021go, zhou2022teleman or sim2real RL~\cite{fu2023deep,liu2024-vbc,ha2024-umionlegs .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our work is based on the low-level controller proposed by VBC~\cite{liu2024-vbc , which designed a bi-level RL paradigm with a low-level whole-body controller.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Notably, some existing work has also attempted to combine teleoperation with whole-body control for quadruped robots~\cite{zhou2022teleman,portela2024-compliant but does not investigate learning skills from teleoperation.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Most related to our work, \citet{ha2024-umionlegs demonstrated whole-body imitation learning with handheld data collection hardware.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The main differences between our work and \citet{ha2024-umionlegs are (1) the data collected without teleoperating the robot can include only wrist camera observation, which may lead to worse performance than multi-camera setup as we empirically verify and (2) \citet{ha2024-umionlegs focus on execution of short tasks; while we investigate in-the-wild mobile manipulation with long horizon task execution.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Sec.~\ref{sec:whole_body_teleop describes adapting a whole-body controller to support efficient teleoperation and more diverse real-world tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In Sec.~\ref{sec:wildact_skill , we propose \acronym{-Skill , which modifies the pre-trained CLIP model~\cite{radford2021-CLIP for generalizable imitation learning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, \acronym{-Planner (Sec.~\ref{sec:wildact_planner ) interfaces \acronym{ -Skill with an LLM planner to carry out long-horizon execution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Recent imitation learning methods have benefited from improved data collection methods via VR/AR-based teleoperation~\cite{cheng2024-opentv,ding2024-bunnyvisionpro,qin2023-anyteleop,he2024omnih2o .}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, though human operators can naturally tele-operate bipedal humanoid robots~\cite{he2024omnih2o,cheng2024-opentv,fu2024-humanplus , it is non-trivial to tele-operate quadruped robots due to the embodiment gap~\cite{padalkar2023-open-X between two-legged human and quadruped robots inspired by four-legged animals.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: To reduce the need for the tele-operator to consider both the base movement and the arm movement, we propose to use a whole-body controller~\cite{liu2024-vbc that allows smooth arm-base coordination for the robot.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In particular, we use the low-level whole-body policy developed by \citet{liu2024-vbc .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Trained with RL, the learned whole-body controller takes in base commands (linear velocity and angular velocity) and 6DOF end effector pose w.r.t.. the arm base.
elements.sent-2411-064: The policy outputs arm and base joint commands for coordinated movement that extends the workspace (illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser_fig ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Based on the pre-trained low-level controller, we then design an interface for human users to teleoperate the robot.
elements.sent-2411-066: We use the OpenTV framework~\cite{cheng2024-opentv with Apple Vision Pro, which allows real-time video streaming, tracking of 6DOF poses of head and hands, and 3D gesture keypoints.}
elements.sent-2411-067: To minimize the expert-agent observation gap~\cite{zhao2023aloha , the tele-operator gets real-time streams of the robot's head camera views and wrist camera views.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.96\textwidth]{imgs/planner_pre_arxiv.pdf} \caption{Overview of \acronym{}-planner. Given a constructed hierarchical scene graph, \acronym{}-planner adopts a coarse-to-fine searching mechanism to determine node traversal and structured actions to take.} \label{fig:WildACT_planner_overview}
elements.sent-2411-068: To translate tele-operator movement to robot movement, we linearly transform the operator's right wrist pose (relative to their initial hand pose) $T_{right \in \mathrm{SE (3)$ into the relative end effector pose $T_{ee \in \mathrm{SE (3)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: We scale the translations with a constant $s_c$, as we find that the workspace of the Z1 arm is slightly larger than average human arms.
elements.sent-2411-070: More concretely, let $\mathbf{R _{right}$ be the rotational component and $\mathbf{t _{right}$ be the translational component of $T_{right}$, $T_{ee}$ is given by, \begin{equation}T_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{right} s_{c} \cdot \mathbf{t}_{right} \mathbf{0}^{\intercal} 1 \end{bmatrix}\,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The gripper open-close actions are then naturally mapped from the pinching of the thumb and the index finger (via 3D keypoints).
elements.sent-2411-072: The whole-body controller automatically controls the base rotation to coordinate with the arm.
elements.sent-2411-073: In turn, the tele-oeprator's left wrist governs planar base movements ({\it e.g., angular and linear velocities).}
elements.sent-2411-074: When the tele-operator pinches their left fingers, VR tracks the pose $T_{left}$ as a virtual joystick with deadzone ($x_{th = 5cm$).}
elements.sent-2411-075: We find this simple base command mapping sufficient for the tasks involved.
elements.sent-2411-076: Many existing methods, however, struggle to generalize to novel environments~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha .}
elements.sent-2411-077: To improve the generalizability of learned skills without expensive demonstration collection cost, we propose to adapt pre-trained CLIP~\cite{radford2021-CLIP to ACT~\cite{zhao2023aloha for imitation learning of individual skills.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We encode camera observations with a frozen CLIP visual backbone.
elements.sent-2411-079: Instead of simply using intermediate CLIP features as in~\cite{chi2024-umi-gripper,ha2024-umionlegs , we apply MaskCLIP~\cite{zhou2022-MASKCLIP , a reparameterization trick to generate image-text cross attention map.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: More concretely, let $\Omega$ be the space of RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-081: The original CLIP~\cite{radford2021-CLIP is a mapping function $f_{visual (\cdot): \Omega \mapsto \mathbb{R ^{C}$, where $\mathbb{R ^{C}$ is the image-text embedding space learned from contrastive learning~\cite{radford2021-CLIP .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: MaskCLIP modifies the network architecture to a new mapping function $g_{visual (\cdot): \Omega \mapsto \mathbf{H \times \mathbf{W \times \mathbb{R ^{C}$, which is a feature map aligned to the CLIP embedding space $\mathbb{R ^{C}$ (illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:clipact_wholebody_overview ).}}}}}}

Both OpenTV and \textit{\acronym{}} achieve noticeably higher success rates in the challenging O.O.D. setting. \textcolor{red}{$^{\dagger}$}: methods involve learned/manual policies that are not trivially applicable to the task settings. \textcolor{red}{$^{*}$}: Method does not differentiate object sets and success rates are averaged on I.D. and O.O.D. object sets.} \label{table:main_results}
elements.sent-2411-083: Consistent with findings by \citet{chi2024-umi-gripper , we found that the adaptation of CLIP~\cite{radford2021-CLIP improves the performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, when tested with objects unseen in the training demonstrations, the success rate is still unsatisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, we propose to make the acquired skills language-conditioned by introducing cross-attention.
elements.sent-2411-086: We provide task-specific texts during both the training and testing time ({\it e.g., for the ADA door button-pressing task, we use `door' and `ADA button') with CLIP text embedding $f_{text (\cdot): Text \mapsto \mathbb{R ^{C}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: With slight abuse of notation, the text vector can then be compared with the CLIP feature map via cosine similarity \begin{equation}CrossAtt(\cdot,\cdot) = \frac{g_{visual}(\cdot) f_{text}(\cdot)}{\left| \left| g_{visual}(\cdot) \right| \right| \left| \left| f_{text}(\cdot) \right| \right|},\end{equation} where the comparison is done independently on the pixel level.
elements.sent-2411-088: The resulting similarity is comparable to the probability map of text queries.
elements.sent-2411-089: We apply dropout~\cite{srivastava2014-dropout to cross-attention during training to avoid over-reliance on attention.}
elements.sent-2411-090: To autonomously terminate skills, in order to hand control back to high-level planners, we add a virtual `end' action signal prediction.
elements.sent-2411-091: Empirically, adding the end signal to only the end of the episode does not work, as the supervision is too sparse.
elements.sent-2411-092: Our proposed solution is to implement a buffer of end signal for every skill such that the last $n=10$ frames of the demonstrations carry the end signal.
elements.sent-2411-093: During deployment, we use a sliding window detector to terminate task execution if the end signal is greater than $\tau = 0.8$ for 10 consecutive predictions.
elements.sent-2411-094: For the base-only skill ({\it i.e., navigating from a known location to another known location), we implement it with analytical planning.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Here, we propose \acronym{-Planner , a simple LLM-based planner to show how learned skills can be composed.}
elements.sent-2411-096: We implement a LiDAR-based SLAM system using FAST-LIO~\cite{xu2021-fastlio and DLO~\cite{chen2022-DLO to obtain consistent robot pose estimation in the world frame.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: We manually annotate pose-level waypoints ({\it e.g., stand in front of receptacles) and connectivity for task execution.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The robot stands at every waypoint to capture images with its head camera.
elements.sent-2411-099: To automatically annotate the semantics of each waypoint, GPT4-V~\cite{openai2023gpt4 provides high-level descriptions of images and lists of objects of each waypoint.}
elements.sent-2411-100: We manually create abstract nodes ({\it e.g., a room with multiple pose-level waypoints) to construct a hierarchical graph for searching.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Note that off-the-shelf scene graph construction methods~\cite{maggio2024-clio,hughes2022-hydra,gu2024-conceptgraphs can potentially replace this step.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We adopt a hierarchical coarse-to-fine approach to translate template-free commands into detailed, actionable robot skills.. Coarse Planner.
elements.sent-2411-103: Using CoT~\cite{wei2022-CoT , the coarse planner receives template-free instructions and decomposes them into individual tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-104: For instance, the command `clean the trash in the hallway' can be decomposed into tasks `navigate to hallway', `pick up the trash', and `place trash in the trash bin'.
elements.sent-2411-105: We will release the detailed prompts.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tabular}{l c c c} Pipeline Collect \& Drop Trash Shelf Rearrangement \acronym{} (Ours) 7/10 3/10 ACT~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,zhao2023aloha} 0/10 0/10 \end{tabular} \caption{Evaluation of long-horizon execution. Given a few training demonstrations (10), \acronym{} improves long-horizon task success rate via (1) improved generalizability of single skill and (2) divide-and-conquer.} \label{table:long_horizon_evaluation}
elements.sent-2411-106: The fine-grained planner invokes actionable skills at particular nodes given individual tasks generated by the coarse planner.
elements.sent-2411-107: The fine-grained planner has prior knowledge of the robot’s skill library (shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:WildACT_planner_overview ) and nodes constructed in the initial mapping stage.}
elements.sent-2411-108: For each task, the agent uses a breadth-first search (BFS) approach to search nodes and identify the optimal goal node.
elements.sent-2411-109: During this stage the LLM acts as a heuristic evaluator, estimating the likelihood of a node being the most likely location related to the task, based on the semantic context and objects present at the node.
elements.sent-2411-110: Once the target node is identified, the planner constructs a plan detailing the navigation and manipulation sequence drawn from the pre-defined skill library.
elements.sent-2411-111: We use the Unitree B1 quadruped robot with a Unitree Z1 arm.
elements.sent-2411-112: We replace the beak-like default Z1 end effector with a 3D-printed parallel soft gripper, which was adapted from UMI-Gripper~\cite{chi2024-umi-gripper to directly operate the gripper with gear rotations.}
elements.sent-2411-113: For perception, an Azure Kinect camera is mounted on the robot's head, and an Intel Realsense D405 is used as the in-wrist camera.
elements.sent-2411-114: A LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR is installed at the robot's tail for enhanced localization during navigation.. Implementation Details.
elements.sent-2411-115: The \acronym{ -Skill module independently trains weights for each skill (with 30-60 demonstrations each acquired via tele-operation).}
elements.sent-2411-116: The head/wrist RGB observations are processed through a CLIP~\cite{radford2021-CLIP ViT-B/16 encoder with MaskCLIP~\cite{zhou2022-MASKCLIP re-parameterization.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Task-specific texts are then compared with the feature maps to generate cross-attention, where texts may differ in training sequences and testing run.
elements.sent-2411-118: For navigation between given waypoints, we implement a PD-based waypoint follower.
elements.sent-2411-119: For efficiency, the spatial locations of nodes are annotated by operating the robot to turn 360 degrees during the initial scene scanning, and the edges are made between physically adjacent nodes with no obstacle in between.

CLIP~\cite{radford2021-CLIP} 83.3\% 69.4\% 76.4\% ResNet~\cite{zhao2023aloha}\textcolor{red}{$^{\star}$} 77.8\% 19.4\% 48.6\% DinoV2~\cite{oquab2023-dinov2} 88.9\% 77.8\% 83.3\% \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation of different visual encoders pre-trained with different objectives. The evaluation is done on the object-grasping tasks. \textcolor{red}{$^{\star}$}: we followed ACT~\cite{fu2024mobilealoha,zhao2023aloha} to use ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-18 as the encoder, which has fewer parameters.} \label{table:backbone_ablation}

Four teleoperators are tasked to manipulate objects at various heights for three trials in each task.} \label{table:wholebody_efficiency_combined}
elements.sent-2411-120: We define two experiment settings to investigate the generalizability of skills learned via imitation learning~\cite{cheng2024-opentv,fu2024mobilealoha,ding2024-bunnyvisionpro .}
elements.sent-2411-121: The in-distribution (I.D.) setting tests the learned skills with backgrounds and object arrangements approximately similar to the training demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that, to make the setting more realistic, we do not enforce identical robot positioning and lighting conditions even in I.D.. settings.
elements.sent-2411-123: The Out-of-distribution (O.O.D.) setting permutes the testing objects (placement/texture), receptacles, and background environments for learned skills.
elements.sent-2411-124: Illustrations of the differences between these two settings can be found on the website.

elements.sent-2411-125: Besides ablating design choices of our components, we implement several baselines to validate the efficacy of \acronym{ .}

elements.sent-2411-141: As an RL-based method, VBC~\cite{liu2024-vbc suffers from sim2real gaps such as inaccurate contact modeling and cumulative sensor latencies.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Therefore, VBC performs worse in real-world settings than its simulation counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-143: On the other hand, zero-shot modular methods such as GeFF~\cite{qiu2024-geff do not naturally exhibit corrective behavior like learning-based methods, which are vulnerable to errors compounding from different modules.}

elements.sent-2411-153: The motivation for combining whole-body control and teleoperation is to improve teleoperation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-154: To validate this point, we report the statistics of teleoperation in Tab.~\ref{table:wholebody_efficiency_combined , which shows our learning-based controller outperforms the decoupled analytical controller from Unitree SDK.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Since these tasks require reaching objects at various heights (toys and books at different levels of storage), teleoperation without whole-body control fails to grasp from the ground due to the limited workspace.
elements.sent-2411-156: Besides qualitative samples in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser_fig , we provide more videos of our robot working on different practical tasks in the supplementary video and the website.}
elements.sent-2411-157: In this paper, we present \acronym{ , a modular framework that includes (1) \acronym{-Skill , which implements a library of generalizable visuomotor skills that improve ACT~\cite{zhao2023aloha for learning generalizable imitation learning skills; and (2) \acronym{-Planner , an interface that enables interactions between imitation learning skills and LLM planner to support long-horizon task execution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Furthermore, we deploy this framework on a quadruped robot controlled by a whole-body controller, which allows us to efficiently collect demonstration data and support extended workspace for diverse tasks.
elements.sent-2411-159: In summary, \acronym{ implements practical, generalizable skills, and long-horizon manipulation, which we hope will motivate future research toward in-the-wild mobile manipulation that facilitates real-world deployment of robots.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Using Chain of Thought~\cite{wei2022-CoT , the coarse planner receives template-free instructions and decomposes them into individual tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-161: For instance, the command `clean the trash in the hallway' can be decomposed into tasks `navigate to hallway', `pick up the trash', `navigate to trash bin', and `place trash in the trash bin'.
elements.sent-2411-162: What advantages does \acronym{-Skill have compared to existing baselines in quadruped manipulation? [A1, A2].}
